title: 5 Hidar — St. Philip the Apostle & The Assembly
narrative:
Hidar

St. Philip the Apostle (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Nahum the Prophet

St. Saba the Sanctified (Sabas of Jerusalem), Monastic Founder

The fifth day of Hidar carries the monthly commemoration of St. Philip the Apostle — Ethiopia's apostolic patron, the one who ran to the chariot — and in the month of the Apostolic Vesting his commission is not merely remembered but renewed. Philip in Hidar is Philip already vested, already running, already seated in the chariot and explaining the scroll of Isaiah. The month commissions the community to do what Philip did: to join itself to the vehicle that is already moving, to recognize the moment of divine appointment when it arrives in the middle of an ordinary road, to provide the word of interpretation that opens the text to the one who has been reading without understanding. The apostolic vesting that Hidar celebrates is not new equipment for new people; it is the renewal of a commission that was always already given — the Philip-commission of the Ethiopian Church, received in the first century and renewed in every liturgical year that passes.

St. Nahum the Prophet brings one of the most geographically specific of all the prophetic books into Hidar's assembly — the oracle against Nineveh, the announcement of that great city's fall. Nahum is often read purely as a message of judgment, but the tradition's inclusion of him in the liturgical calendar is not primarily for the judgment it announces but for the theological principle underlying it: that the powers that oppress the covenant community do not have the final word, that their apparent invincibility is a temporary condition, that the divine initiative which creates and sustains the community is more durable than the empires arrayed against it. In the apostolic month of Hidar, Nahum's oracle is the prophet's contribution to the community's commissioning: go, not because the empires have already fallen, but because the One who sends you has already determined that they will.

St. Saba the Sanctified — Sabas of Jerusalem — was the fifth-century founder of the great laura of Mar Saba in the Judean desert, the monastery that has survived to the present day and that became one of the most important centers of Eastern Christian monastic life, liturgical development, and theological formation in the history of the Church. Sabas was a man of extraordinary ascetic gifts and extraordinary communal organizing ability — a combination that is relatively rare in the tradition, where the great contemplatives are often poor administrators and the great administrators are often not deep contemplatives. He managed to be both: the founder of multiple monasteries, the organizer of the Palestinian monastic federation, the defender of Chalcedonian orthodoxy, and simultaneously a hesychast of profound interior depth. His apostolic vesting combined the interior gifts of Tiqimt with the community-building vocation of Hidar in a single life.

Philip, Nahum, and Saba together on the fifth day of Hidar present the apostolic vesting across three different scales of commission. Philip works at the individual scale — one chariot, one official, one baptism, one community begun. Nahum works at the imperial scale — his oracle is addressed to the greatest city of the ancient Near East, and its message concerns the movements of divine justice through the largest structures of human power. Saba works at the institutional scale — founding communities that will outlast him by centuries and that will produce the liturgical and theological tradition that still shapes the Eastern Church today. The apostolic vesting of Hidar equips for all three scales, and the community entering its fifth day is being prepared for the particular scale to which its own commission is addressed.
